coredata.dc:title: Core@shell quantum dots as a fluorescent probe for the detection of cholesterol and heavy metal ions in aqueous media
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Mir, Irshad Ahmad
Kumar, Sachin
Bhat, Masroor Ahmad
Yuelin, Xie
Wani, Aijaz Ahmad
Zhu, Ling
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, CuInS2@ZnS (CIS@ZnS) core-shell quantum dots (QDs) were applied for the detection of heavy metals and bioanalytes. These Glutathione (GSH) capped CIS@ZnS QDs with an average size of 3.8 nm were treated using different biologically important biomolecule analytes, such as ascorbic acid (AA), citric acid (CA), oxalic acid (OA), cholesterol (Ch), cysteine (Cy), dextrose (Dx) and urea (U). According to spectroscopic analysis, the addition of cholesterol showed photoluminescence (PL) spectra of QDs remains unchanged at the main peak position 600 nm but generates a new peak at 487 nm with cholesterol dependent enhancement. Furthermore, the addition of citric acid contributes to PL quenching in the core-shell QDs without any spectral shift. Likewise, the interaction of CIS@ZnS QDs with different physiologically important heavy metal ions (HMI) was systematically studied as a luminescence probe. It was found out that the PL emission of CIS@ZnS QDs was effectively quenched by copper (Cu2+) ion with significant spectral shift detected. However, Hg2+ ions also lead to PL quenching at nanomolar level but with no spectral shift observed. According to these results, CIS@ZnS QDs are selectively sensitive to cholesterol, citric acid, Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions with satisfactory detection limit at nM level.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Core-shell quantum dots
Bioanalytes
Heavy metal ions
Fluorescence sensing
Limit of detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection analysis as a spectroscopic response

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the grants from Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation , China (Grant no: 2019A1515011762 ), Shenzhen Science and Technology Innovation Foundation , China (Grant no: JCYJ20180305125302333 , JCYJ20170818093035338 , JCYJ20180305125430954 ), Shenzhen University Fund , China (Grant no: 860-000002110229 ) and Foshan City Education Department Foundation , China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) PL excitation and emission spectra of CIS and CIS@ZnS core-shell QDs at different Cu/In ratios (1/2, 1/3, 1/4, 1/5 and 1/7). (B) PL excitation and emission spectra of CIS and CIS@ZnS QDs under the optimal conditions (Cu/In~ 1/4), (C) XRD of CIS and CIS@ZnS core-shell QDs (D) Photographs of QDs in day light and UV excitation and (E and F) TEM and size histograms (Inset) of CIS and CIS@ZnS core-shell QDs respectively.
XPS spectra of the MW synthesized CuInS2@ZnS core-shell structures. Elemental identification scans of (A) Cu 2p, (B) In 3d, (C) S 2p, and (D) Zn 2p of the core-shell QDs.

(A) PL spectra of CIS@ZnS (5 µM) QDs with the increased concentration of cholesterol, (B) Intensity ratio at 487/ 600 nm verses cholesterol concentration, (C) FWHM and area under the PL curve at different cholesterol concentrations, (D) Interference pattern in presence of other analytes at the same concentration of 100 nm. (E) Plot for the determination of LOD and (F) Decay time measurements of CIS@ZnS QDs in the presence of Ch (200 nM, 400 nM and 600 nM).

(A) PL spectra of CIS@ZnS (5 µM) QDs with the increased concentration of citric acid, (B) Stern Volmer plot, (C) FWHM and area under the PL curve at different citric acid concentrations, (D) Interference pattern in the presence of other analytes at the same concentration of 100 nm. (E) TRFS measurements of CIS@ZnS QDs in the presence of CA (200 nM and 400 nM).

PL spectra of CIS@ZnS 5 (µM) QDs, with the increased concentration of (A) Cupper, (B) Mercury, (C) Stern Volmer binding plot CIS@ZnS QDs binding with metal ions, (D) Integrated area under the PL curve against metal concentration. (E) FWHM of QDs at different concentrations of metal ions. (F) Wavelength shift of QDs with the increased concentration of metal ions.

head.ce:title: Core@shell quantum dots as a fluorescent probe for the detection of cholesterol and heavy metal ions in aqueous media

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of optoelectronic devices and systems, Ministry of education / Guangdong Province, College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, Southeast University, Nanjing, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Govt. Degree College Kulgam, Jammu and Kashmir, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, Govt. Degree College Kulgam

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Govt. Degree College Kulgam, Jammu and Kashmir, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, CuInS2@ZnS (CIS@ZnS) core-shell quantum dots (QDs) were applied for the detection of heavy metals and bioanalytes. These Glutathione (GSH) capped CIS@ZnS QDs with an average size of 3.8 nm were treated using different biologically important biomolecule analytes, such as ascorbic acid (AA), citric acid (CA), oxalic acid (OA), cholesterol (Ch), cysteine (Cy), dextrose (Dx) and urea (U). According to spectroscopic analysis, the addition of cholesterol showed photoluminescence (PL) spectra of QDs remains unchanged at the main peak position 600 nm but generates a new peak at 487 nm with cholesterol dependent enhancement. Furthermore, the addition of citric acid contributes to PL quenching in the core-shell QDs without any spectral shift. Likewise, the interaction of CIS@ZnS QDs with different physiologically important heavy metal ions (HMI) was systematically studied as a luminescence probe. It was found out that the PL emission of CIS@ZnS QDs was effectively quenched by copper (Cu2+) ion with significant spectral shift detected. However, Hg2+ ions also lead to PL quenching at nanomolar level but with no spectral shift observed. According to these results, CIS@ZnS QDs are selectively sensitive to cholesterol, citric acid, Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions with satisfactory detection limit at nM level.

Schematic depiction CIS@ZnS QDs sensing for Bioanalyte and HMI
ga1

ce:section.ce:para: Also known as quantum dots (QDs), luminescent nanocrystals have attracted a lot of attention as a stable, nontoxic material for the sensing of chemical and biological analytes [1–5]. The nano-sensors with high sensitivity, excellent selectivity and a low limit of detection (nanomolar) are much needed in both medical science and health care for the evaluation of physiological and metabolic parameters. The highly sensitive and specific sensing of biologically important analytes, for example, cholesterol, citric acid, uric acid, dextrose, ascorbic acid, cysteine etc., is essential for understanding the pathological and physiological processes of humans [6]. Among these biomolecules, cholesterol is of much biological significance to human health [7–10]. Cholesterol, as an essential element present in human blood, is responsible for the development of cell membranes and the production of some essential biomolecules including vitamin D, bile acids and steroid hormones. The normal level of cholesterol for health adults ranges reportedly from 2.9 to 6.0 mM [11]. A high level of cholesterol in bloodstream increases the risk of developing coronary heart disease [9,12]. In 2008, it was found out that 17.3 million people died from cardiovascular diseases, accounting for 30% of global deaths. This number is predicted to reach approximately 24 million in 2030 [13]. Thus, the quantification and determination of cholesterol concentration in bloodstream is extremely challenging for both researchers and the health care industry. In this context, cholesterol biosensor has attracted widespread attention from across the world for the improvement of their sensing ability. Nanotechnology provides enormous opportunities to develop the cholesterol biosensors with improved efficiency, sensitivity, specificity and stability.

As another variety of biologically important analyte, citric acid (CA) has been commonly used as an antibacterial, buffering and flavor agent in foods, beverages, cosmetic products and the pharmaceutical industries [14]. Known as an effective immunity booster, citric acid plays a role in countering the attacks from bacteria and virus. Citric acid is effective in reducing the symptoms associated with noroviruses [15]. Potassium citrate as a derivative of CA is crucial for preventing the development of kidney stones and breaking down those already present [16]. To date major health issues in children (one in 33) is urinary stones [17]. In this sense, determining the level of cholesterol and CA is conducive to minimizing such health-related risks and issues, which is essential for public health.

In addition to these bioanalytes, the heavy metal ions beyond the permissible limit are detrimental to both humans and aquatic life. HMI-contaminated drinking water has already been known as the cause of various diseases that pose threat to human healthy [18–20]. In some studies, it has been demonstrated that the PL intensity of QDs changed significantly in the presence of such metal ions as Cu2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Fe2+ and Ag+ [21–25]. This prompts researchers to explore the development of fast and cheap sensors for the detection of toxins, heavy metals and other industrial pollutants. As the major toxic metal ions among all the heavy metal ions, mercury can cause severe damage to the biological system at both cellular and molecular levels. These metal ions are the causative agent for various acute and chronic symptoms of poisoning in humans, including abdominal pain, skin cancer, renal failure and liver impairment [26,27]. Among the heavy metals, cupper (after Fe3+ and Zn2+) is the most common metal present in human body. The normal range of blood copper for healthy people is 100–150 μg/dL [28]. The imbalance of cupper can lead to various disorders, including Parkinson disease [29] and cancer [30]. The deficiency of Cu(II) can result in Menkes disease [31], while excess Cu(II) can lead to Alzheimer’s and Wilson’s disease [32]. The higher level of Cu2+ ions in the environment increases cupper level in the bloodstream, thus resulting its deposition in brain, cornea and kidney, which is damaging to the nervous system [33,34]. Therefore, the detection of cupper ions has become an urgent biomedical and environmental problem.

The health risks associated with these toxic metals and bioanalytes have underlined the necessity to develop an effective solution to the rapid detection of metals at extremely low concentrations, in ground water and soil samples. At present, there have been various analytical methods developed for metal sensing, such as atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), atomic fluorescence spectrometry (AFS), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), and electrochemical sensing platforms [35]. Though these traditional methods are highly effective in detecting heavy metal ions, they remain disadvantaged by complex instrumentation, long sample preparation and purification time, as well as high cost. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a cost-effective, simple and rapid solution to the detection of heavy metals for assisting the real-time monitoring of environmental samples.

The nanomaterials synthesized using less toxic precursors provide an eco-friendly solution for various applications [36–41]. Over the last decade, various nanomaterials have been applied to design the highly efficient biosensors for detecting biomolecule analytes [1,2,4,6,23]. As nanotechnology advances, it is necessary to develop novel methods of producing highly selective and accurate devices for proficient point-of-care testing with high efficiency and cost effectiveness for the healthcare industry. Among a variety of different sensors, fluorescent and ratiometric fluorescent sensors have attracted a great deal of attention for analytical sensing due to their technical simplicity, cost effectiveness, the ease of use, real-timeness, as well as high selectivity and sensitivity [5,6,42,43].

The discovery of quantum dots (QDs) has contributed significantly to accelerating the research on producing low-cost materials, for the detection of tumor, toxin, heavy metal ions, bioanalytes and biomolecules [6,25,44]. Despite the effectiveness of Cd-based quantum dots in heavy metal detection, their toxicity is a major drawback, which limits its environmental and biomedical applications [45,46]. Recently, it has been demonstrated in some studies the CIS-based QDs are applicable for tumor targeting and bio-imaging [44], which prompts our attempt to explore the sensing capability of Cd free CIS based core-shell QDs. With non-toxic Cd free QDs as the focus, CIS-based core-shell QDs were taken as an effective fluorometric probe for the detection of bioanalytes (such as cholesterol and citric acid) and toxic heavy (Cu2+ and Hg2+) metal ions. It was found out that CIS@ZnS core-shell QDs exhibits selectivity and specificity in the sensing of Ch, CA, Cu2+, and Hg2+. To the best of our knowledge, there is still no report on the use of CIS@ZnS QDs as a fluorescence probe for the detection of Ch, CA, Cu2+ and Hg2+.

The experimental details and synthetic process are detailed in Electronic Supplementary Information (ESI) section. The synthesis was carried out according to the report given ref. [40], with some significant modifications made. In this study domestic microwave oven used in the previous report [40], designed for kitchen purposes was replaced by a dedicated microwave reactor known as MAS-II Plus microwave, designed by SINEO Microwave Chemistry Technology Co., Ltd,. Scientifically microwave synthesis should be carried out using dedicated instrumentation that was designed with some specific features such as safety over the temperature range, controls the Possibility of superheating, Excellent parameter control, Stirring, Continuous power output and Software control. MAS-II Plus is designed with the dual-channel temperature detection system used to detect the temperature through PT thermocouple sensor as well as non-contact infrared ray. Designed with closed-loop control PID technology that allows the precise adjustment of temperature, this system is fitted with a color screen to display the whole reaction processes, temperature and other parameters in real time during the reaction process. It adopts two stirring methods that can be switched at any time, mechanical stirring and magnetic stirring. The stirring speed can be adjusted continuously. Some operations can be carried out during the reaction process, such as reflux condensation, dropping liquid and water diversion, etc. Theoretically, the operating temperature of the equipment can reach up to 900 ℃.

In comparison with household MWO, the existing model shows an improvement of PL quantum yield (QY), stability, and minimum size deviation to a large extent. Given the disadvantages of domestic microwave oven, a comparative analysis was carried out to reveal that the existing model can also produce quantum dots with higher stability and enhanced photophysical properties. Through MAS-II Plus, different parameters of synthesis were optimized corresponding to higher PL intensity and stability. The different optimizations for the synthesis of QDs were noted as (pH as 8.7, microwave power as 300 W, irradiation time as 330 s and reaction temperature as 100 0C. In comparison with domestic microwave synthesis, the PLQY was found to improve by 25%. Additionally, tunable emission properties were achieved corresponding to different cation ration (Cu:In) at a fixed value of Cu+In content (shown in Fig. 1(A)). The highest PLQY and stability was achieved for Cu:In ratio as 1:4. Though the ionic radii of copper and indium is basically consistent, which is 77 pm and 80 pm respectively, the two cations display clearly different properties, because of the mismatched bond lengths of Cu-S and In-S, which results from some intrinsic defects within the QDs [47,48]. Due to their large surface to volume ratios, QDs have relatively significant surface defects as the main source of radiative and nonradiative e-h recombination process [49]. Consequently, these turnery structures exhibit low PLQY but large emission FWHM. Thus, the surface passivation of core with a shell having relatively more band gap such as ZnS etc., that eliminates the nonradiative surface dangling bonds and thus enhances the PLQY of CIS QD [41,50,51]. Furthermore, the stability (against oxidation/ aggregation) and luminescence properties of core QDs were improved by coating ZnS shell over core QDs. Notably, the intensity of excitation and emission peak showed red shift with the increased content of Cu/In. This significant shift towards the lower energy is attributed to the improvement of maximum valence band, without any changes to size in the QDs [47]. It has been suggested in study that increasing Cu content could enhance the Cu d- orbital properties, thus leading to the Cu d-/S p- interband repulsion that causes the elevation of valance band maximum [47].

Similar to other reports [52], the absorption spectra of QDs exhibited no distinctive absorption peak, as shown in Fig. S1(A). Therefore, the PL spectroscopy was used to identify the excitation wavelength needed to detect the corresponding emission peak. As shown in Fig. 1(B), CuInS2 displayed PL excitation and emission peak at 450 nm and 620 nm respectively. However, the core-shell QDs showed blue shift with a significant increase in emission intensity and the corresponding excitation/ emission peaks were located at 430/ 600 nm, respectively. It was found out that the core only QDs were least stable under normal atmospheric conditions, while core-shell QDs were highly stable under similar conditions. The occurrence of shift in the bandgap energy of core-shell QDs towards higher energy (Fig. 1(B)) resulted from the formation of core-shell. The PLQY of these aqueous dispersed QDs was calculated using Rhodamine 6 G in ethanol as our standard having its reported PLQY as 95%. The PLQY equation used here is expressed as,
(1)
Q
Q
=
Q
R
I
Q
I
R
A
R
A
Q
η
Q
η
R
2
Where, Q, I, A and η represent the quantum efficiency, integrated emission intensity, absorption edge value, and refractive index of the solvent, respectively. The subscript “R” stands for reference (Rhodamine 6 G) and “Q” refers to quantum dots. Time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy (TRFS) was taken as another important optical tool to characterize semiconductor QDs. The excited-state lifetime of the emission changed significantly while shifting from core to core-shall QDs. Fig. S1(B) shows the decay curves well fitted to a bi-exponential equation (Eq. (S1), given in ESI), with the average decay time obtained as 280 and 369 ns for CIS and CIS@ZnS nanocrystals, respectively. The calculated data is shown in Table S1.

As shown in Fig. 1(C), XRD profile shows the broad peaks caused by the small crystallite size of particles. The 2θ diffraction peaks of CIS QDs were observed at around 27.87°, 46.28°, and 54.97°, which can be assigned to (112), (024), and (116) tetragonal planes of CIS structures, respectively (JCPDS no.32–0483). Besides, XRD 2θ peaks CIS@ZnS QDs shifted slightly towards higher values as compared to tetragonal CIS, as a result of the core-shell structures. CIS exhibits tetragonal crystal structure while ZnS usually demonstrates hexagonal/or cubic crystal properties [30,31]. Thus, the peaks of core-shell structure shifted slightly from CIS core and approached those of ZnS structures (JCPDS no. 65–0309), as located at 28.61°, 47.59°, and 56.47° corresponding to (111), (220) and (311) cubic planes respectively. Different synthetic processes produced similar results for the homogenous (CIS)x(ZnS)1−x, alloyed CIZS and core-shell structures [21,24,30–35]. For this reason, it is insufficient to prove the formation of core-shell QDs only through XRD analysis. However, synthetic process is the most significant parameter for the formation of different structures. Generally in core-shell formation, shelling over the core follows the growth of core structures with the addition of shell precursors [24,31–33]. Therefore, XRD along with spectroscopic properties (PLQY enhancement/blue shift, life time measurements), and most importantly synthesis procedure, formation of core-shell structures could be proved.

The photographs of these aqueous stable QDs in day light and under UV illumination are shown in Fig. 1(D), as obtained under optimal experimental conditions. TEM was used to determine the average size and shape of QDs. Fig. 1(E and F) shows the TEM images of CIS and CIS@ZnS core-shell QDs with an average particle size of 3.3 and 3.8 nm, respectively.

Furthermore, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was adopted for elemental iddentification. According to the XPS data shown in Fig. 2(A), there were two characteristic peaks exhibited at 932.5 eV and 952 eV associated with Cu 2p 3/2 and Cu 2p 1/2, respectively. Similarly, two distinct peaks for In 3d 5/2 at 445 eV, In 3d at 3/2452 eV (Fig. 2(B)) and a single peak of S 2p 1/2 at 161.5 eV, shown in Fig. 2(C). Fig. 2(D) shows two peaks corresponding to Zn 2p 3/2 and Zn 2p 1/2 located at 1021 eV and 1044 eV, respectively. Thus, Cu 2p, In 3d, S 2p and Zn 2p core levels are scrutinize, respectively. Therefore, the presence of all the elements was detected as expected.

Additionally, the confirmation of the presence of capping agent on the surface of QD is of utmost importance. Thus, FTIR spectra shown in Fig. S1(C) are compared for GSH only and GSH-QDs systems. We noted that absorption stretching N–H bands for free GSH occurs at 3120 cm−1 and 3021 cm−1. The bands at 2517, 1638 and 1531 cm−1 are assigning for stretching vibration of –SH band, stretching vibration of CO bond and distortion vibration of NH bond in amide groups, respectively. On the contrary, the absence of–SH vibration band at 2517 cm−1 and the disappearance of distortion N–H band in QDs confirms the presence of GSH on the surface of the QDs via the SH and NHR groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection analysis as a spectroscopic response
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, Cd free CuInS2 based CuInS2@ZnS core-shell QDs are proposed as a platform for the sensing of bioanalytes (cholesterol and citric acid) and HMI (Cu2+ and Hg2+). Due to the high stability and excellent luminescent properties of these QDs, sensing was monitored by spectroscopic techniques such as UV-Vis, PL and TRFS. Notably, core only QDs are less stable than core-shell QDs under normal atmospheric conditions. Therefore, core-shell QDs were used for the sensing of HMI and bioanalytes. We further investigated the feasible value of pH for the best sensing performance of QD over a range of pH values (5.5–11.5). The quantum dots showed aggregation when the pH value fell below 5.5. Therefore, this pH value was treated as the minimum pH level for this study. By observing the absorption curve of QDs (Figs. S (3,7 and 8)), it can be found out that there was an increase in the absorbance value from the baseline with the decrease in pH value, which is similar to the report of Gregory et al. [53].

The PL sensing of analytes was performed with the addition of various commonly-used bioanalytes such as AA, CA, OA, Ch, Dx, Cy and U in the aqueous dispersion of CIS@ZnS QDs at a single excitation wavelength of 430 nm. To demonstrate the usability of QDs for the sensing of analytes, the PL response of the QDs at different concentrations of bioanalytes was recorded, as shown in Fig. (3 and S2). The experimental results suggested the presence of cholesterol generates new peak (at 487 nm) in the emission of QDs, which is attributed to the formation of QD-Ch complex. It is noteworthy that, the PL intensity of QDs increased progressively at the new emission peak (487 nm), while the main QDs peak at 600 nm decreased slightly in the PL intensity corresponding to the increased concentration of Ch (Fig. 3A). The ratio of the intensities at the two distinct peaks allows us to provide a ratiometric sensing probe the detection and quantification of Ch level. Consequently, the PL intensity ratio of QDs (I487/I600) increased continuously in the presence of Ch from 0 to 120 nM (Fig. 3B). A clearly linear relationship was observed in the concentration range from 0 to 120 nM with a correlation coefficient of 0.99 (Fig. 3B). The main focus of analyte sensing is placed on selectivity and sensitivity. Therefore, the selectivity and sensitivity of QDs to cholesterol was assessed through parallel measurements, where the PL response of the QDs to other analytes was observed at 487 nm and other data including FWHM, integrated area and LOD was analyzed. As shown in Fig. 3 C, the addition of Ch caused some changes to the integrated area and full width half maxima (FWHM) of QDs emission peak centered around 600 nm. As shown in Fig. 3D, there was no change in the PL intensity of QDs at 487 nm due to other analytes. In addition, the LOD was calculated from the Fig. 3(E) using the Equation S4 (given in ESI) and the value was estimated to be 10 nM. Therefore, the PL enhancement of QDs at 487 nm in response to the different concentration of cholesterol contributes to the selective detection of cholesterol. Thus, the specific sensing of Cholesterol can be assisted by this analysis of generation of new peak and corresponding ratiometric changes in PL intensity/decay time, absorption value and the changes in integrated area/ FWHM.

Moreover, UV-Vis spectroscopic data suggests that the presence of Ch changed the absorption spectrum significantly. As shown in Fig. S(3), the absorption value increased linearly with the concentration of Ch, thus providing an absorbance dependent sensing platform for the determination of Ch level. It is suggested that the formation of complex between GSH capped QDs and Ch that results the absorption changes. For this reason, this absorption data was used to determine the association constant Ka using the Fig. S3(F), according to the Benesi-Hildebrand equation (Eq. (S2), given in ESI). These values are listed in Table S2. Despite significant difference in association constant over the pH range (5.5–11.5), pH 8.5 shows a slightly higher value of association constant as compared to other pH values. Additionally, the PL intensity of GSH capped QD was found to be the highest at the pH value of 8.5 during the synthesis optimizations. This suggests that QDs are most stable at this pH value and therefore, the pH value of 8.5 is considered the best for the sensing system.

The sensing mechanism of the QD-Ch system was further studied through the TRFS measurements of the QDs in the presence of three different concentrations of Ch samples (100, 200,300 nM), as shown in Fig. 3(F). The decay time of QDs changed from 369 ns to 419 ns and then to 442 ns after the addition of 100 nM and 200 nM quantity of Ch, respectively. In comparison, the decay time profile shown in Fig. 3(F) indicates that the life time was reduced sharply to 338 ns when 300 nM Ch was added into QDs solution. Based on these results, the changes of decay profile with at lower/ higher concentration of Ch could be attributed to the following reasons. Firstly, since the QDs contain a large number of surface trap states over a wide range of energies and structures [54,55], it is suggested that the Ch binds with the QDs surface through the amino group, thus minimizing the QD surface defect states that generally produce nonradiative recombination. Ultimately, nonradiative paths would be subdued [56]. Secondly, since the PL properties of QDs are largely determined by the severity of aggregation, it is suggested that the addition of Ch may cause aggregation for the QDs so that a generation of new peak can be observed. There might be potential aggregation, because the optical view of the QDs samples was slightly changed after the addition of Ch. Furthermore, the increase in Ch content led to surplus packing and the trapping of photogenerated hole in thiol ligands, as a result of which the PL life time of GSH capped QDs was shortened [57–59].

Notably, it was discovered that the PL intensity of QD declined on a continued basis with the increase in CA concentration ( Fig. 4 A) but there were only insignificant changes observed in the presence of other bioanalytes such as AA, OA, Dx, Cy and U, etc. (Fig. S2). Therefore, a further analysis was carried out using the PL emission data of QDs in the presence of CA. The quenching data was used to find the binding parameters according to the Stern Volmer binding relationship (Eq. (S3)) shown in ESI [42,60]. The Stern Volmer plot shown in Fig. 4B shows the graph of log [(I0 –I)/I] vs log [Q], which could be used to determine the binding constant K as ≈ 1.7 × 109 M of QD-CA system, from the slope and intercept.

Furthermore, the integrated area of QDs emission spectra varies significantly with the addition of CA, while full width half maxima (FWHM) shows no significant change (Fig. 4C). To demonstrate the excellent sensing ability of QD probe for CA, other analytes such as AA, OA, U, Ch, Dx and Cy, at the same concentration of 100 nM, were used to assess their impact on the PL emission through an interference test for CA. As shown in Fig. 4D, in comparison to other analytes only CA causes a significant reduction to PL intensity suggesting that QD probe has a high selectivity to CA. Further investigation for the sensing mechanism was performed by UV-Vis spectroscopy and PL life time measurements. The UV-Vis data shown in Fig. S4 suggests no change in the absorption spectra of QDs in the presence of CA over a pH range of (5.5–11.5). In contrast, decay time measurements shown in Fig. 4(E) exhibit a reduction to life time of QDs in the presence of CA. From Table S3, it can be seen that the decay time of the QDs was reduced from 369 to 351 and then to 335 ns after the addition of CA concentration as 200 and 400 nM, respectively. Literature suggested that the PL quenching mechanism could be attributed via static quenching [61], dynamic quenching (energy or photoinduced electron transfer (PET)) [62–64] and inner filter effect (IFE) [62,65]. The static quenching arises due to the formation of nonfluorescent ground state complexes and produces the changes the absorption spectra [61]. But we didn’t note any change in absorption of QDs in presence of CA, which undoubtedly excluded the presence of static quenching. IFE quenching generally occurs without any change in life time of fluorophore in presence of analyte due to no excited-state energy/electron transfer [66]. Thus, the decrease in the life time of the QDs in presence of CA also excludes the IFE sensing strategy. Therefore, dynamic quenching (energy/electron transfer) is only the possible quenching mechanism for CA-QD system. One important requirement for the energy transfer is that the absorption spectrum of the acceptor must overlap the fluorescence emission spectrum of the donor and thus removes the possibility of energy transfer in CA-QD system. Therefore, PET is only possible quenching mechanism for QD-CA system. On the other hand, PET arises from electron transfer in the excited-state follows the changes in the lifetime of the fluorophore. The decrease in decay time and the confirmed unaltered absorption spectra of QDs in presence of CA have definitely demonstrated that the mechanism of PL quenching should be attributed to the PET. Additionally, citrate acid is known as a tricarboxylic, the strength of its binding is reasonably high and it consists of a strong coordination of the carboxyl oxygens to the QDs surface [67], which also may be the cause for the binding of QD-CA system.

In addition to bioanalytes sensing, a probe was developed for the detection of heavy metal ions, as executed by PL measurements ( Fig. 5, S5 and S6). The QDs were treated using various metal ions such as arsenic, cadmium, cobalt, copper, Iron, lead, mercury, nickel, vanadium and zinc. The metal ions were used with a minimum concentration of 10 nM. PL Quenching was observed at nM level for the tested metal ions such as copper and mercury (Fig. 5). Differently, cadmium, cobalt, arsenic, nickel, iron, lead, vanadium and Zinc were not observed in nanomolar detection (Figs. S5 and S6). The probe revealed that the emission intense peak was quenched continuously with the selective addition of HMI, such as Cu2+and Hg2+, as shown in Fig. 5A and B, respectively. Apart from PL quenching, the PL measurements suggested the emission red-sift (600–645 nm) with the increscent addition of Cu2+ ions (0–150 nM), as shown in Fig. 5 (A and F). More importantly, the LOD was estimated based on the emission linear fitting through Eq. (S4) and the values was calculated to be 0.22 nM. These limits are far lower than the limit of copper (~20 µM) in drinking water, as reported by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency [68]. Therefore, our QD provide a sensing probe for metal detection which is in compliance with the WHO standard for drinking water. Also, our QD probe is highly sufficient and sensitive to detect Copper ion in living cells. As shown in Fig. 5B, its PL intensity was reduced gradually without changing its λ em when Hg2+ of different concentrations was added with the fixed amount (5 µM) of core-shell QDs, which evident the intrinsic PL quenching (PLQ) of Hg (II). The lower detection limit was found to be 5 nM for mercury ions. As shown in Fig. S6, also PL quenching of QDs was noticed with the addition of Cadmium and Lead ions, but the detection is limited to the micro molar level. Therefore, it can be concluded that only Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions were sensitive for nanomolar detection. In order to assess the ability of the sensing system, selectivity is regarded as a critical parameter. To demonstrate the high selectivity of QD sensing platform for Cu2+and Hg2+ metal ions and their interference impact on each other, a comparison was performed of different parameters such as binding parameters, integrated area, FWHM, and the shift in emission peak intensity. The binding constant (K) of QD-Cu2+ complex was determined from the Stern Volmer binding plot Fig. 5C and the value was calculated to be 7.3 × 109 M−1. The binding value resulting from the complexation of QD-Hg system was expressed as 1.5 × 109 M−1. The excellence of sensor in Cu2+ detection was also visualized by the comparative plot of integrated area verses metal concentration, as shown in Fig. 5D. At the same concentration of 80 nM, cupper ions showed a 90% decrease in the PL integrated area as compared to 55% caused by Hg2+ ion. Given these facts, CIS@ZnS core-shell QDs were proposed as a sensing platform for the detection of Cu2+ and Hg2+ metal ions with different quenching parameters. As shown in Fig. 5E, Cu2+ ions caused more significant changes to the FWHM of QD PL spectra than Hg2+. Besides the above analysis, it was also noticed that only Cu2+ ions displayed red-shift in the emission (λmax) of core-shell QDs which was found to be 25 eV (Fig. 5F), while the Hg2+ ion showed no significant shift in λmax.

It is known that the QDs surface covered by the ligands provides different functional groups (hydroxyl, carbonyl and amino groups), through which the QDs can bind with the metal targets resulting the PL quenching. As discussed above different possibilities could produce PL quenching of QDs in contact of CA. Therefore, UV-Vis and TRFS experimental data were analyzed for further investigation. As shown in Fig. S8, the absorption value increased linearly with the concentration of Cu2+ ions, thus providing the evidence for the formation of nonfluorescent ground state complexes. It can be observed that Cu2+ ions reduced the life time of QDs sharply (Fig. S7/Table S4), suggesting the PL quenching via energy or electron transfer. Through the paramagnetic ion, the Cu2+ with d9 system that has one unoccupied d shell could effectively quench the fluorescence of nanaparticles through electron transfer [69,70]. Herein, GSH capped QDs have these functional groups with negative surface potential [40] and Cu2+ acts as an electron accepter. As a result, FL quenching was observed through the formation of QD- Cu2+ complex and the charge transfer between QDs and Cu2+ [62,71]. The presence of Cu2+ ions leads to red shift in the emission peak ranging from 600 nm to 650 nm, which is suspected to result from the chelation of Cu2+ ions via the carbonyl groups present on the surfaces of the QDs [62,71]. Thus it is concluded that the presence of Cu2+ ions produces PL quenching of QD via the formation of nonfluorescent ground state complexes as well as electron transfer between QDs and Cu2+.

Moreover, it is known that Hg2+ could be tightly bound with carboxyl, thiol, or amido functional groups, thus resulting in the formation of insoluble HgS ultrasmall on the surface of QDs [72,73]. Therefore, Hg2+ could bind with GSH capped QDs tightly to form HgS particles, which is effective in quenching the PL of QDs through the non-radiative recombination of excited electrons hole pair[72,74].

The CuInS based core-shell QDs synthesized in an eco-friendly way to show high stability were prepared using the MAS-II Plus microwave designed by SINEO Microwave Chemistry Technology Co., Ltd. More importantly, a simple yet efficient method was developed for the detection of cholesterol, citric acid, cupper and mercury through CuInS2 @ZnS quantum dots. Notably, the addition of cholesterol gave rise to a new peak at 487 nm with cholesterol dependent enhancement. In comparison, there was slight quenching in the main peak at 600 nm, which enabled the development of a ratiometric sensing probe for Ch detection. Moreover, CA contributed to the florescence quenching in the core-shell QDs via electron transfer, without any spectral shift. The excellent detection limit of ~10 nM and binding constant of 1.7 × 109 M−1 were obtained for QD-CA system. Furthermore, Cu2+ ions showed PL quenching of QDs due to the combined effect of formation of nonfluorescent ground state complexes as well as electron transfer between Cu2+ and QDs. The extremely low detection limits of 0.2 and 5 nM were observed for Cu2+ and Hg2+, respectively, suggesting that the glutathione capped CuInS2@ZnS QDs were highly sensitive to these metal ions. A binding constant of 1.5 × 109 M−1 was estimated for the QD-Hg2+ system. To the best of our knowledge, these CuInS2@ZnS core-shell QDs are the first system proposed for the sensing of cholesterol, citric acid, Cu2+ and Hg2+ metal ions. The simplicity, rapidity and cost-effectiveness of this system make it highly efficient for the detection of heavy metal ions and bioanalytes. According to these results, CIS@ZnS QDs were highly sensitive to cholesterol, citric acid, Cu and Hg at nM level.

I. A. Mir, M. A. Bhat, X. Yuelin worked out on almost all the experimental measurements, I. A. Mir performed in all the data analysis along with A. A. Wani. I. A. Mir contributed to the conceptualization of the methodology for the study, drafted and revised the manuscript critically for important intellectual contents. This study was conceptualized by I. A. Mir, S. Kumar and Ling Zhu. All authors have provided final approval of the manuscript to be published.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the grants from Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation, China (Grant no: 2019A1515011762), Shenzhen Science and Technology Innovation Foundation, China (Grant no: JCYJ20180305125302333, JCYJ20170818093035338, JCYJ20180305125430954), Shenzhen University Fund, China (Grant no: 860-000002110229) and Foshan City Education Department Foundation, China.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photophysical properties of biologically compatible cdse quantum dot structures

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.A. Kloepfer, S.E. Bradforth, J.L. Nadeau, Photophysical Properties of Biologically Compatible CdSe Quantum Dot Structures, J. Phys. Chem. B. 109 (2005) 9996–10003. doi:10.1021/jp044581g.

Shen, Study of Fluorescence Enhancement of Colloidal CdSe/ZnS Quantum Dots Bound to Hexadecylamine by Single-Molecule Measurements, J. Phys. Chem. C. 111 (2007) 15166–15172. doi:10.1021/jp074561p.
